coredata.dc:description: On a global scale, approximately 1 in 8 (13.6 %) of worldwide mortalities have been associated with bacterial infections. Therefore, the necessity for rapid and cost-effective diagnostic kits is paramount to accurately diagnose bacterial infections. Besides, the utilization of IgY antibodies emerges as a natural and economical option for effective sensing of pathogens. Notably, electrochemical IgY-based immunosensors amalgamate the inherent specificity of immunoreactions with highly sensitive electrochemical sensors, thereby facilitating the advancement of next generation Point-of-Care (POC) devices. In this work, a label-free IgY-based immunosensor for the detection of E. coli, employing Boron and Nitrogen-doped Graphene Quantum Dots (B, N-doped GQDs) nanomaterial as the electrode interface was successfully devised. This developed immunosensor has demonstrated commendable sensitivity of 0.0395 µA. (CFU/mL)−1 & 0.0491 µA. (CFU/mL)−1 from Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) & Amperometry (Amp) techniques across various E. coli concentrations (0.5 x 101 – 107 CFU/mL) and exhibited exceptional selectivity of < 5.88 %, thereby ensuring precise E. coli detection while mitigating interference from other agents. Moreover, real sample analysis with the developed sensor displayed exemplary recoveries, affirming its efficacy in detecting E. coli. The immunosensor’s promising results in terms of its figures-of-merit underscore the potential diagnostic utility of IgY in E. coli screening bacterial sensing applications.

head.ce:title: Chicken IgY based electrochemical immunosensor for the detection of bacterial pathogen E. coli in water

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: On a global scale, approximately 1 in 8 (13.6 %) of worldwide mortalities have been associated with bacterial infections. Therefore, the necessity for rapid and cost-effective diagnostic kits is paramount to accurately diagnose bacterial infections. Besides, the utilization of IgY antibodies emerges as a natural and economical option for effective sensing of pathogens. Notably, electrochemical IgY-based immunosensors amalgamate the inherent specificity of immunoreactions with highly sensitive electrochemical sensors, thereby facilitating the advancement of next generation Point-of-Care (POC) devices. In this work, a label-free IgY-based immunosensor for the detection of E. coli, employing Boron and Nitrogen-doped Graphene Quantum Dots (B, N-doped GQDs) nanomaterial as the electrode interface was successfully devised. This developed immunosensor has demonstrated commendable sensitivity of 0.0395 µA. (CFU/mL)−1 & 0.0491 µA. (CFU/mL)−1 from Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) & Amperometry (Amp) techniques across various E. coli concentrations (0.5 x 101 – 107 CFU/mL) and exhibited exceptional selectivity of < 5.88 %, thereby ensuring precise E. coli detection while mitigating interference from other agents. Moreover, real sample analysis with the developed sensor displayed exemplary recoveries, affirming its efficacy in detecting E. coli. The immunosensor’s promising results in terms of its figures-of-merit underscore the potential diagnostic utility of IgY in E. coli screening bacterial sensing applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Escherichia coli (E. coli), a widely distributed fecal coliform bacterium that is present in the intestines of both humans and animals, can be utilized as a dependable indicator of water contamination caused by sewage or animal waste [1]. The rapid transmission of bacterial infections occur through various routes, such as airborne droplets, dust, direct and indirect contact, vectors (e.g., ticks or mosquitoes), and contaminated food or water [2]. Consequently, bacterial infections can lead to severe illnesses such as meningitis, septicemia, intestinal infections, etc. Certain populations, notably infants, young children, and the elderly, are particularly vulnerable to the fatal effects of the E. coli O157:H7 strain. In the clinical diagnosis of bacterial pathogen infections, standard techniques involve the analysis of biological samples, such as blood, sputum, urine, stool, tissue, cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), and mucus (from the nose and throat), using staining analysis with a microscope, bacterial culturing, and assessing sensitivity and susceptibility to antibiotics and antimicrobial drugs [3]. Additionally, multi-pathogen molecular panels that rapidly identify the genetic characteristics of the organisms can expedite the determination of infection sources compared to conventional culture methods, which often take days. Nevertheless, these diagnostic methods have limitations in terms of feasibility, speed of analysis, and technical complexity. Moreover, accurately distinguishing between false positive and false negative results in mixed cultures poses a considerable challenge [4].

Numerous rapid sensing technologies employ antibodies as a means of pathogen detection, thereby achieving desirable selectivity. The development of immunosensors has been documented to represent a significant advancement in sensing capabilities due to their enhanced binding capacity between bacterial antigens and specifically immobilized antibodies. Nonetheless, the utilization of such specific antibodies necessitates costly preparation steps, thereby augmenting the overall expenses of real-time sensor applications [5]. Various methods for measuring immunosensor signals include electrochemical, optical, piezoelectric, acoustic, and thermometric techniques. Electrochemical immuno-sensing, in particular, holds promise for facilitating the development of point-of-care (POC) devices, as it effectively combines the intrinsic specificity of immunoreactions with the exceptional sensitivity of electrochemical sensors, thus serving as a viable strategy for investigating immunocomplex formation and enabling accurate diagnosis [6]. A review focusing on the electrochemical biosensing of Escherichia coli O157:H7 has underscored recent advancements in this field, highlighting the urgent need for swift and cost-effective diagnostic kits to achieve accurate diagnosis of bacterial illnesses. The timeframe of 2015–2023 has seen considerable progress in the utilization of electrochemical immunosensors for E. coli detection. These sensors predominantly employ monoclonal (specific to a single type of antigen) or polyclonal (specific to multiple antigens) IgG antibodies sourced from Rabbit or mouse. However, as an alternative to IgG, avian egg yolk antibodies (IgY – Immunoglobulin Y), which possess natural properties and can be feasibly prepared, present a viable and effective sensing factor for the detection of diverse pathogens.

Egg yolk antibodies (IgY) represent natural immunoglobulins elicited by a specific antigen to induce an immunological response, derived from the egg yolk of avian species. These IgY antibodies find widespread application in research, diagnosis, and treatment. Unlike IgG, IgA, and other commonly utilized antibodies originating from mammals, such as rodents, rabbits, and mice [7], the production of antibodies in mammals involves vaccination, blood sample collection, and sacrifice [8], inflicting pain and distress upon the animals. Despite using a substantial number of animals for antibody production, the quantitative yield of IgG in mouse plasma remains at 7–10 mg/mL. Consequently, there is a pressing need for an economical and efficient approach to reduce animal usage and associated suffering. This has spurred the advancement of the emerging IgY technology, which circumvents blood collection by producing IgY within the eggs of avian, reptilian, amphibian, and lungfish species, thereby significantly decreasing animal usage [9]. Moreover, the increased yield achieved through this technique enhances the cost-effectiveness of production, as it is directly associated with the quantity of antigen immunized, its immunogenicity, and molecular weight [10,11]. Acknowledging the discomfort induced by invasive blood collection, the European Centre for the Validation of Alternative Methods (ECVAM) workshop advocated the use of IgY over mammalian IgG in 1996 [12]. Although IgY-based immunosensors have been less explored in research [13–15], they have demonstrated excellent specificity and proven efficacy in pathogen and allergen detection, as well as cancer diagnostics.

Besides antibodies, nanomaterials are employed in sensors, offering several advantages in electrochemical sensing applications. The surface modification of the electrode through nanomaterials yields enhanced sensing performance. Notably, conductive nanomaterial-based modifications involving graphene, nanotubes, and fullerenes are commonly utilized to amplify the sensor's signal. Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) are nanocrystals with less than 100 nm lateral dimensionsmade of one to multiple layers of graphene sheets [16]. GQDs exhibit exceptional qualities, propelling advancements in biosensors and bioimaging research [17–19]. The specific characteristics of GQDs, such as a high surface area-to-volume ratio, favourable electron donor and acceptor ability, and the presence of hydrophobic edges and hydrophilic planes, enhance the attachment of biomolecules to the surface of electrode, thereby improving the electrochemical signal − a crucial factor in immunosensor development. GQDs contain various functional groups, including carboxylic, hydroxyl, and epoxy moieties, augmenting their dispersibility in water. Doped GQDs offer enhanced optical and electronic properties, tunability such as bandgap, conductivity, and energy levels, by adjusting the type and concentration of dopants, improved stability, enhanced catalytic activity, and improved biocompatibility compared to pristine GQDs [20], graphene [21], GO [22], and rGO [23]. GQDs can be created using both top-down and bottom-up techniques; however, the top-down approach is often preferred due to its ability to produce stable and homogenous size and shape QDs [24]. In this study, we propose the synthesis of GQDs using the precursors citric acid, boric acid, and urea via a straightforward and less complicated pyrolysis-based carbonization approach [25]. The utilization of citric acid, boric acid, and urea as sources of carbon, boron, and nitrogen, respectively, leads to the creation of boron, nitrogen doped GQDs (B, N-doped GQDs), which exhibit superior electrocatalytic activity compared to regular GQDs. The introduction of dopants into the GQDs network is facilitated by the difference in the electronegativity between the nitrogen (χN = 3.04) and carbon atoms (χC = 2.55) and thus provisions for substitutional doping into the GQD framework [26]. Moreover, heteroatom co-doping is a useful tactic in enhancing the total active sites. Also, within the planar structure, boron acts as a stable and suitable candidate for substitutional doping [27]. Additionally, because of their similarities to carbon in terms of size and valence electron, boron and nitrogen can be incorporated into GQDs more easily. The planar GQDs' structure is strengthened by positively charged boron sites on N's pyridinic nature. Nitrogen-doped GQDs also demonstrate greater specific energy, power, and cycling stability (80 % capacity retention) than GQDs and B-doped GQDs, making B, N-doped GQD a more favourable nanomaterial for sensing applications [28]. A study [29] suggested that among the hybrid and mono doped graphene better electrochemical performance was observed for B, N-Graphene, which affirms the potentiality of B, N GQD towards electrochemical applications.

However, another study by S. Xiao et al [13] used metal covalent organic frameworks m-COF microbeads to label the IgY as capture probes forming a sandwich type immunosensor for recognizing E. coli. Magnetic covalent organic framework (m-COF) microbeads represent a promising platform for immunosensors, yet they face challenges hindering their widespread adoption. Synthesis complexity poses a significant hurdle, demanding precise control over reaction conditions, potentially limiting scalability and reproducibility. Despite their high surface area, m-COFs may offer limited sites for biomolecule immobilization due to their structural characteristics, impacting assay sensitivity. Moreover, m-COF stability under various conditions is crucial, ensuring sustained performance over time. Magnetic interference, arising from the microbeads' properties, poses another challenge, potentially complicating assay readouts. Addressing these challenges through ongoing research efforts is paramount. Streamlined synthesis protocols, surface modifications enhancing biomolecule immobilization, and robust regeneration strategies are critical areas for advancement.

Direct label-free Immunosensor with doped GQD as a novel and suitable substrate for IgY immobilization while also improving E. coli detection. This is the first paper to talk about GQD for IgY immobilization for detecting E. coli. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) represent a novel and promising advancement in immunosensors, offering several distinct advantages. GQDs possess excellent electrical and optical properties, including high quantum yield and large surface area-to-volume ratio. This enables efficient signal transduction, leading to enhanced sensitivity in detecting analytes, even at low concentrations. GQDs biocompatibility ensures compatibility with biological samples, allowing for precise functionalization with biomolecules like antibodies without compromising assay accuracy. Versatility in functionalization permits tailored detection strategies, enhancing specificity across a broad range of analytes. Moreover, GQDs exhibit robust stability under varying environmental conditions, ensuring consistent performance over time. Cost-effectiveness in GQD synthesis and scalability enhances their viability for commercial applications, fostering widespread adoption. Collectively, these attributes position GQDs as versatile and promising tools for advancing immunosensor technologies [30]. The primary focus of this work lies in the detection of E. coli for diagnosing bacterial infections using a simple, low-cost IgY-based electrochemical immunosensor with easy preparation and uncompromised sensitivity, which is highly demanded in the field of diagnostics.

Citric acid, boric acid, chitosan, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), and sodium hydroxide were procured from Sigma Aldrich, India. Urea was acquired from Fisher Scientific, India. Ferricyanide [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− was obtained from Merk Life Science, India. The necessary chemicals for PBS preparation were sourced from Sigma Aldrich, India. Pesticides, including profenofos, imidacloprid, and carbendazim, were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, India. Additionally, other ionic interferents such as sodium bicarbonate, zinc acetate dihydrate, lead nitrate, chromium trioxide, and cupric acetate were also acquired from Sigma Aldrich.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of B, N doped GQD
ce:section.ce:para: B, N-doped GQDs were synthesized utilizing the pyrolysis-based carbonization technique as outlined in scheme 1 . The B, N GQD preparation protocol was similar to [25] but with a slight modification such as using hot plate for direct pyrolysis instead of a blast drying oven. The characterization results of the prepared B, N GQD was comparable with the above mentioned similar study [25]. This suggests that the pyrolysis-based carbonization method for B, N GQD synthesis is efficient and reproducible. This synthesis approach involved dissolving 0.5 g of citric acid, 0.3 g of urea, and 0.1 g of boric acid in 5 mL of deionized water followed by thorough mixing using a sonicator for 15 min. Subsequently, the mixture was subjected to heating at 200°C on a hot plate for a duration of 2 h. The observation of changes in solution colour (from colourless to yellowish-black) over time served as significant indicators of GQD formation within the solution medium. Sampling was conducted at two time points, namely 30 min and 2 h, to facilitate a comparative assessment of the pyrolysis effect with respect to time. Upon cooling the synthesized mixture to room temperature, approximately 10 mL of deionized water was introduced, and NaOH was added to the reaction mixture to bring the pH down to 7 or 8. The resulting solution was subsequently transferred to centrifuge tubes, and 5 mL of deionized water was employed to collect the sediments in the beaker. The subsequent steps involved centrifuging the solution three times at 10,000 rpm, each lasting 20 min, at a temperature of 4°C, with the aim of removing sediments effectively. Finally, the obtained solution was subjected to dialysis using a dialyzing membrane (3 kDa) with 2 mL of the sample dialyzed against 250 mL of deionized water for a duration of 1 h to eliminate impurities. The dialyzed 30 min & 2 h B, N-doped GQDs samples were preserved at 4°C for subsequent applications.

For the purpose of bacterial sensing, a bacterial culture was generated in Luria Bertani (LB) broth. A volume of 5 µL of E. coli culture was introduced into 10 mL of autoclaved LB broth and subjected to overnight incubation at 37°C. Subsequently, the broth underwent triple centrifugation and was subsequently washed with 10 mM phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). The resulting pellets were re-suspended in PBS to yield the stock solution, and their optical density (OD) was assessed using a UV spectrophotometer. The prepared E. coli stock solution was determined to possess 109 colony-forming units per milliliter (CFU/mL). To obtain diverse concentrations of E. coli (101 –108), the stock solution was subjected to dilution in PBS. In order to validate the concentration levels, the plate count method was employed to verify the viability of the various bacterial stock solutions.

All animal experimental protocols were reviewed and approved by Institutional Animal Ethics Committee (CPCSEA Approval Number: 595/SASTRA/IAEC/RPP). White leghorn layer female chickens (19 weeks old weighs about 1.2Kg) were purchased from commercial breeders and placed in individual cages. All the animal experiments were comply with the ARRIVE guidelines. The IgY preparation protocol was adapted from the seminal work of Polson et al. [31,32] with slight modifications. The isolation of immune egg shells was executed with utmost care. Delicate cracking of the egg shells enabled the collection of the yolk, which was subsequently placed on a paper surface to eliminate excess albumin. Subsequently, the yolk was gathered in a centrifuge tube, post the removal of the yolk membrane. To achieve the desired final volume, 3.5 % polyethylene glycol (PEG 6000) and twice the amount of PBS were introduced, followed by thorough mixing on a roller mixer for 20 min to ensure homogeneity and attain the final volume. The resultant mixture was then filtered through Whatman No. 1 filter paper after being centrifuged at 10,400 rpm for 20 minat 4°C. Subsequently, 8.5 % PEG 6000 was incorporated into the filtrate using the same process. Centrifugation was used to obtain the final pellet, which was then diluted with PBS to a volume of 10 mL. The addition of 12 % PEG 6000 was then repeated in the process. Following the combination of the last pellet with 800 mL of PBS, the mixture was transferred into an activated dialysis bag. The dialysis process was carried out overnight in saline with PBS changes performed hourly for 3 h, ultimately yielding a dialyzed volume of approximately 2–2.5 mL. The quantification of total protein and total IgY was conducted using UV spectroscopy [33]. Moreover, the estimation of antigen-specific IgY titer was performed using indirect ELISA (section 3.3). IgY production is reproducible, with the similar antigen preparation, immunization protocols, standardizing egg collection and processing, and implementing consistent purification methods, we can get approximately the same amount of antigen specific antibody (Anti-E. coli IgY). Quality control measures, including titer assessment and validation assays, ensure uniformity between batches. Despite variations in bird health and environmental conditions, meticulous attention to detail enhances the reproducibility of IgY antibody production.

Firstly, the glassy carbon electrode was subjected to deionized water washing, and subsequently, the working electrode surface underwent alumina powder polishing, followed by rinsing with deionized water. Subsequently, a mixture of 100 µL of B, N-doped GQDs was homogenized with 100 µL of chitosan (a binder molecule), and 4 µL of the resulting interface material was coated onto the glassy carbon electrode, left for drying under optical illumination. The surface-modified electrode was immersed in 300 µL of 2 mM EDC and 5 mM NHS for 30 min at room temperature, facilitating the activation of –COOH group. Secondly, 10 µL of chicken Anti-E. coli polyclonal IgY antibody (1 mg/mL) was covalently immobilized onto the electrode, and an incubation period of 40 min (optimized within the range of 10–60 min) was maintained within a humid chamber at 4°C, after which washing with deionized water ensued. Furthermore, the electrode underwent immersion in 300 µL of 1 % Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) for the purpose of blocking any remaining binding sites, followed by a 15 min incubation period, and subsequent washing with PBS and deionized water. The modified glassy carbon electrode can be stored at 4°C for future utilization.

The optical analysis was conducted using the Thermo Scientific UV–visible spectrophotometer (model: Evolution 201). In order to comprehend the chemical functionalities involved in the preparation of B, N-doped GQD, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometry was employed. The structural analysis was performed utilizing the D8 Focus X-ray diffractometer from Bruker, Germany, which was equipped with a Cu Kα source (λ = 1.5408). The morphological characteristics were observed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with VEGA3, TESCAN instruments. A CHI600E electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments) was used for the electrochemical sensing experiments, with a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) serving as the working electrode, platinum wire serving as the counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl saturated in KCl serving as the reference electrode.

The optical characteristics of the prepared B, N-doped GQDs were analysed through UV–Visible spectroscopy of the samples pyrolyzed for 30 min and 2 h (Fig. 1 (a)). The obtained UV–Visible spectrum (Fig. 1(a)) exhibited a distinct absorbance peak at 336 nm, which confirmed the presence of B, N-doped GQDs and indicated the successful preparation of the compound. Additionally, the 230 nm absorption peak was caused by the sp2 carbon's π-π* transition, while the superior recognition of the n-π* transition pointed towards the presence of functional groups like oxygen [34,35]. The band gap energy of the B, N GQDs for both the 30 min and 2 h samples was determined using Tauc's plot method, by extrapolating the linear curve and identifying the x-axis intercept, resulting in approximately 4.12 eV and 4.16 eV, respectively (Fig. 1(c) and (d)). This analysis indicates that the synthesized B, N-doped GQDs possesses discrete energy levels and exhibits properties of small-sized quantum dots. Remarkably, the band gap energy of the synthesized B, N-doped GQDs was barely affected by the longer pyrolysis time.

The photoluminescence spectrum was utilized to investigate the fluorescence properties of B, N-doped GQDs, as depicted in Fig. 1(b). The emission characteristics of the 2 h and 30 min pyrolyzed B, N-doped GQDs samples were examined, revealing maximum emission wavelengths of 433 nm and 430 nm, respectively. Furthermore, an optimal excitation wavelength of approximately 310 nm was identified. The introduction of nitrogen and boron dopants in GQDs induced variations in the GQDs' particle sizes, causing the emergence of distinctive surface states in proximity to the conduction band. These surface states were responsible for the observed photoluminescence behaviour of B, N-doped GQDs. Additionally, the time-dependent effects during the pyrolysis process contributed to the growth in GQDs' size, widening of their fluorescence peak, and an increasing reliance on fluorescence emission. Remarkably, the carbonization process using citric acid also became more pronounced. Consequently, the 2 h pyrolyzed B, N-doped GQDs exhibited a considerably intensified emission spectrum [36,37].

In order to evaluate the functional groups and chemical bonds present in the synthesizedB, N-doped GQDs (Fig. 2 (a)), FTIR analysis was used. The presence of the O-H and N-H stretching vibrations is evident from the well-defined peak observed at 3426 cm−1 [25]. Moreover, the occurrence of C-H groups within the GQD structure is substantiated by the characteristic peaks detected at 2848 cm−1 and 2920 cm−1. Likewise, the presence of an alkene bond is deduced from the minor peak observed at 2357 cm−1, affirming the existence of C=C stretching vibrations. Additionally, the peak detected at 1718 cm−1 corresponds to the N-C=O stretching, thereby signifying the inclusion of nitrogen groups in the GQD matrix [25]. Furthermore, the peak at 771 cm−1 provides evidence of secondary amine (N-H) presence in the synthesized material. The peaks found at 1631 cm−1, 1398 cm−1, and 1179 cm−1 arise from the C=O stretching, B-O stretching, and B-O-H bending vibrations, respectively, which convincingly demonstrates the incorporation of boron within the synthesized GQDs [25]. Finally, the presence of C-O/C-O-C stretching is indicated by the peak at 1093 cm−1. In summary, the FTIR results unequivocally confirm the presence of boron and nitrogen in the B, N-doped GQDs framework.

According to the findings reported by [38], the diffraction peak observed at an angle of 27.5° corresponds to the crystallographic plane characteristic of graphene. The XRD pattern of B, N-doped GQDs is depicted in Fig. 2(b). The carbonization of citric acid through pyrolysis led to the formation of the graphite structure, as evidenced by the presence of the graphene plane. Additionally, the B, N-doped GQDs exhibit similarities with both graphene and GO, as documented in the literature [39]. The considerable intensity and broadness observed in the aforementioned diffraction peak can be attributed to the high degree of crystallinity and small particle size of the B, N-doped GQDs, respectively [40].

The Fig. 3 presents SEM images of B, N-doped GQDs acquired at diverse magnifications. The emergence of nanoclusters attests to the efficacy of the modified synthesis approach in facilitating the successful generation of B, N-doped GQDs. As the physical and chemical properties of nanomaterials can be tuned by controlling their shape and size at the nanometer scale, enabling novel applications [41]. This study has featured Boron and Nitrogen doped GQD to have good conductive and electrocatalytic activity enabling it to apply as a transducing material to amplify electrochemical signal for sensing [42]. Notably, these spherical nanostructures exhibit amplified surface area and elevated catalytic functionalities, rendering them potentially valuable for sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of Anti E. coli IgY

ce:section.ce:para: To assess the specificity of the generated Anti E. coli IgY towards E. coli and other bacterial antigens, an Enzyme Linked Immunosorbent Assay (ELISA) was conducted. It also shows the importance of the affinity or avidity of the developed IgY antibody to the E. coli antigen. The in-house readily available P. aeruginosa whole cell antigen was employed in this assay to evaluate the selective binding of Anti E. coli IgY with P. aeruginosa. First, 96-well microtiter plates were coated with 100 µL of each whole cell antigen from P. aeruginosa and E. coli, and they were left to sit overnight at 4°C. Subsequently, the plates were washed thrice with 0.05 % Tween 20 (PBST), followed by the addition of blocking solution PBS and 5 % bovine serum albumin, and further incubated for 2 h at 37°C. After another wash with PBST, serially diluted IgY antibodies were added to the plates and incubated overnight at 4°C. The plates were cleansed thrice with PBST, and 100 µL of 1:5000 HRP-conjugated Rabbit anti-IgY antibody was added, incubating for 1 h at 25°C. Following another round of washing with PBST, each well received 100 L of TMB substrate, which was then incubated at 25°C for 30 min. The colour development was halted by adding approximately 2 N H2SO4 stopping solution, and the absorbance was measured at 405 nm [43]. Fig. 4 depicts the graphical representation of the obtained results. The results indicate that IgY successfully identifies and binds to the E. coli antigen even at higher dilutions. Moreover, due to the presence of surface antigens resembling those on E. coli, the Anti E. coli IgY exhibits recognition of P. aeruginosa at a 1:1 IgY dilution. However, at higher dilutions, IgY fails to identify P. aeruginosa which proves that the developed IgY is E. coli specific.

As depicted in Fig. 6 , cyclic voltammetry (CV) was employed to analyse the electrochemical characteristics of the surface modified GCE. At each stage of the assembly process, the electrode surfaces underwent cyclic voltammetry while employing a 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− redox probe mediator species. All readings were conducted in a 0.1 M KCl and 10 mM PBS solution, utilizing a scan rate of 10 mV/s with the potential vary from + 0.9 V to −0.9 V. Notably, the voltammograms for all electrodes exhibited distinct anodic and cathodic peaks owing to the electron transfer phenomena in the ferricyanide species. For the bare GCE, [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− displayed a defined voltammogram’s response however, upon modification with B, N-doped GQD, the electrode demonstrated a reduced peak separation and an increased faradaic current in comparison to the bare GCE, signifying the effectiveness of the modified material. Upon activation with EDC/NHS, the Ep decreased, and the faradaic currents increased. This electrochemical behaviour could be attributed to the coupling of EDC-NHS with the unpaired carboxyl groups on the electrode surface, thereby weakening the repulsive force between the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− anions and the free carboxyl groups. Moreover, the covalent immobilization of anti-E. coli IgY onto the functional surface of B, N-doped GQD@GCE resulted in a lower peak current. Additionally, the presence of unbound portions of IgY on the modified electrode was effectively obstructed by the blocking effect of BSA. The immobilized antibodies acted as a non-conductive organic barrier, impeding electron transfer and the diffusion of the redox mediator [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− to the electrode surface, paving to a simultaneous widening of the anodic and cathodic peaks. In contrast, the introduction of E. coli antigen and the formation of an immuno-complex at the electrode surface resulted in a smaller faradaic current. These findings provide evidence of the establishment of an immunocomplex at the electrode surface and the successful immobilization of antibodies.

One of the pivotal determinants influencing the effectiveness of the immunosensor pertains to the temporal aspect of coupling amino groups of anti-E. coli IgY with the activated B, N-doped GQD@ GCE surface. This particular parameter underwent optimization using the CV technique, and all experimental procedures were conducted within a solution containing 0.1 mM KCl, 5 mM redox probe [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, and 10 mM PBS. The applied potential underwent sweeping from + 0.9 to −0.9 V, at a scan rate of 10 mV/s. The acquired plot indicates a positive correlation between immobilization time and the binding of IgY, thereby resulting in a concomitant reduction in faradic current. Interestingly, an incubation period of 40 min was identified as the optimum duration, beyond which no discernible changes in the peak current were observed. This observation suggests that, during this incubation period, a substantial amount of carboxyl groups on the electrode surface remain available for binding with anti-E. coli IgY (Fig. 7 (a)).

Under the conditions of an ideal experimental setup, the optimization of the immunosensor's operating pH was achieved using the CV technique, leading to an enhancement in the sensor's sensitivity. The experimental procedures were conducted within a 0.1 mM KCl solution carrying 5 mM of the redox probe [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and 10 mM of PBS. The investigation encompassed a scan rate of 10 mV/s and a potential vary from + 0.9 to −0.9 V, covering the pH vary (4–11), taking into account the stability of IgY. Despite observing elevated current responses under acidic conditions, a pH of 7 was selected as the optimized pH due to the neutral pH range (7–8) typically found in water samples. Notably, under basic conditions (pH 8–11), a constant response was depicted in Fig. 7(b). Consequently, all electrochemical immunosensor assessments were conducted at the optimized pH of 7.

To analyse the immunosensor's performance metrics, CV was conducted using varying concentrations of E. coli under optimized experimental conditions. A series of E. coli solutions (varying from 101 to 107 CFU/mL) were prepared by combining 10 mM PBS with 0.1 M KCl and 5 mM of the redox probe [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in an electrolyte solution. The voltammetric analysis encompassed three trials for each standard E. coli solution, covering a potential vary from + 0.9 to −0.9 V at a scan rate of 10 mV/s. As predicted, as the concentration of E. coli rose, the anti-E. coli IgY − EDC/NHS − B, N-doped GQD@GCE immunosensor's response decreased due to the formation of immunocomplexes on the electrode surface. Fig. 8 (a) displays the representative voltammograms, accompanied by the respective linear regression equation of log I (µA) = -0.0395 log E. coli + 1.0896 and a correlation coefficient of 1. Notably, the immunosensor's current response exhibited a linear relationship with the logarithm of E. coli concentration within the range of 0.5 × 101 to 107 CFU/mL (Fig. 8(b)). Through calculations, the limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantification (LOQ) (using the following relations) of the developed sensor were determined to be 1.69 and 5.75 CFU/mL, respectively.

where, s denotes the standard deviation of the blank responses and m denotes the slope of the linear regression line.

Amperometry, in conjunction with voltammetric investigations, was employed for the analysis of the specificity of the developed Anti E. coli immunosensor. The experimental conditions were optimized, and multiple measurements (three times) of each E. coli solution (varying from 0.5 × 101 to 107 CFU/mL) were conducted in an electrolyte comprising 10 mM PBS (pH 7.2) and 0.1 M KCl, supplemented with 5 mM of the redox probe [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. The detection potential was fixed, and the duration of the experiment was set to 100 s, encompassing the concentration range from 0.5 × 101 to 107 CFU/mL (Fig. 8(c)). A linear regression equation, log I (µA) = -0.0491 log E. coli + 0.5973, with a correlation coefficient of 0.99 (Fig. 8(d)) was established. Based on calculations, the LOD and LOQ were determined to be 1.71 and 5.94 CFU/mL, respectively. By a comparative evaluation of the figure-of-merits between the analysis techniques voltammetry and amperometry employed for the concentration study. As a result of low LOD and LOQ with good sensitivity, cyclic voltammetry was adopted as the standard technique for further sensing analyses. Comparing the obtained results (Table 2 ) with data from the literature, it becomes evident that the proposed method offers comparable or superior attributes, including a wide linear range and an excellent limit of detection. Despite the existence of detectors in the literature with lower LODs, the analytical effectiveness of the novel device in analyzing water samples is more than satisfactory.

Common elements across these methods include surface modification of electrodes with various nanomaterials or biochar, functionalization with specific antibodies or antigens, and the utilization of electrochemical techniques for detection. Researchers [44,46,49] utilize different nanocomposites or nanoparticles, such as ZnO-CuO NC composite, AuNPs, and AuNP-mediated electron transfer, to enhance sensitivity and specificity. They demonstrate detection limits of 2, 15, and 100 CFU/mL. Studies [13,45,48] involve the use of biochar, magnetic covalent organic framework microbeads, and hybrid bio-nanocomposites, respectively, for immobilizing antibodies and capturing target bacteria offer detection limits of 3, 4 log, and 30 CFU/mL. These works [47,50] describe the conjugation of nanoparticles with antibodies for capturing and detecting E. coli, with detection limits of 10 and 3.8 CFU/mL respectively.

Overall, these approaches showcase the versatility of nanomaterials and surface modification techniques in enhancing the performance of immunosensors for E. coli detection using methods like voltammetry, amperometry and impedimetric. While the specific materials and methods vary, all aim to achieve high sensitivity, specificity, and quick detection, demonstrating potential applications in food safety and healthcare settings. Our work focuses on simple label free direct immunosensor with B, N doped GQD as nanomaterial for IgY immobilization and able to display a higher sensitivity with low detection limit. This electrochemical immunosensor can be a potential sensor for E. coli detection in water samples.

By analyzing the cyclic voltammetric response of 104 CFU/mL E. coli, the repeatability assessment of the developed sensor was carried out. This evaluation was carried out by recording the current response of same modified immunosensor over five consecutive cycles, under uniform conditions as shown in Fig. 9 (a) (Electrolyte: 10 mM PBS with 0.1 M KCl and 5 mM of the redox probe [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−; potential range from + 0.9 to −0.9 V; scan rate of 10 mV/s). The observed relative standard deviation (RSD) discovered to be lower than 0.2 %, thus signifying a satisfactory level of repeatability in the output of the immunosensor. In order to assess the immunosensor's selectivity, electrochemical analysis was conducted to evaluate its response to potential interferents within the examined sample. The concentrations utilized for the interferents were as follows: bacteria − Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), Salmonella enterica (S. enterica) at 106 CFU/mL; pesticides − carbendazim, profenofos, imidacloprid at 1 µg/mL; anion − bicarbonate (HCO3 −) at 1 µg/mL; and cations − copper ion (Cu2+), chromium ion (Cr3+), zinc ion (Zn2+), lead ion (Pb2+) at 1 µg/mL.

The CV signals were recorded for the analyte and interferents when all chosen substances were included in the PBS solution, which contained 104 CFU/mL of E. coli. Based on the results obtained (Fig. 9(b)), it can be deduced that despite these compounds being present at 100-fold higher concentrations than E. coli in the model solutions, their impact on the immunosensor response was minimal, with a percentage inhibition of less than 5.88 %. Further, in the absence of E. coli the interferents study was conducted, under similar experimental conditions (Fig. 9(c)). The overall percentage inhibition caused by the potential interferents for the developed immunosensor without E. coli was computed to be 5.37 %. Consequently, the immunosensor exhibited outstanding selectivity towards the analyte even in the presence of potential interferents and displayed excellent performance under interferents scenarios.

The reproducibility assessment of the fabricated sensor was conducted by analyzing the CV data acquired from 104 CFU/mL E. coli using five distinct GCE configurations. Also, the five distinct GCE configurations were tested against multiple times prepared B, N-doped GQDs nanointerface (R1, R2, R3: represents different times prepared B, N-doped GQDs nanointerface), while maintaining uniform experimental conditions (Fig. 9(d)). Notably, all employed electrodes exhibited analogous current responses, thereby attesting to the reproducibility of the developed immunosensor with %RSD of about 2.13 %. The developed immunosensor stability was assessed by examining the CV response of 104 CFU/mL E. coli over a duration of 5 consecutive days under identical experimental conditions (Fig. 9(e)). The relative stability percentages demonstrated on subsequent days were 99.68 %, 96.54 %, 85.33 %, and 70.34 %, respectively, with respect to the initial day's current response. These findings suggest that the Immunosensor can effectively maintain its operational efficiency for up to 3 days when stored at 4°C.

To evaluate the operational efficacy of the newly devised sensor within authentic environmental conditions, three distinct water sample types − namely, reverse osmosis (RO), untreated, and treated water samples − were employed for the empirical analysis. Preceding their utilization, the samples underwent filtration through a 0.22 µm cellulose syringe filter. Tripartite measurements were conducted for each sample, and the outcomes have been succinctly presented in Table 3 . The discerned concentrations and recovery investigations convincingly demonstrate the precision and dependability of the established methodology encompassing the fabrication process and detection mechanism, enabling the accurate detection of E. coli in water samples.

All animal experimental protocols were reviewed and approved by Institutional Animal Ethics Committee (CPCSEA Approval Number: 760/SASTRA/IAEC/RPP). BALB/c mice (female) weighing about 20–30 g were obtained from Central Animal Facility, SASTRA Deemed to be University. All the animal experiments were comply with the ARRIVE guidelines. Fresh faecal samples were collected from four different BALB/c mice and serially diluted using sterile saline. Then, the serially diluted faecal samples were subjected to plate count to determine the endogenous E. coli load in the faecal of normal mice. To assess the ability of Anti-E. coli-IgY for detecting endogenous E. coli through ELISA, all the serially diluted faecal samples were aliquoted into two parts; then to make spiked faecal samples ∼ 1 x 106 CFU of E. coli cells were added to one part and another part of the diluted faecal samples were used as such. At the end, all the faecal samples were put through to indirect ELISA.

In the plate count analysis, E. coli growth was observed on the selective EMB media during the serial dilution plating of the fresh faecal samples. The plate count results showed that two samples (A and B) had too numerous to count (TNTC) colonies at the 10−1 dilution, while the other two samples (C and D) had TNTC colonies at both the 10−1 and 10−4 dilutions. The growth of E. coli is too numerous due to the overnight growth in the EMB media. Besides, the serially diluted faecal samples were subjected to the indirect ELISA, the results demonstrated that the anti-E.coli-IgY antibody (titer 1:16,000) was able to detect the presence of E. coli in all the fresh faecal samples as shown in Table 4 .

Furthermore, the ELISA results also showed that the anti-E.coli-IgY antibody was able to detect the E. coli in the spiked faecal samples, where ∼ 1 x 106 CFU of E. coli cells were added to one part of the diluted faecal sample. These findings suggest that the anti-E.coli-IgY is sensitive and reliable protein for the detection of E. coli in both direct (non-spiked) and spiked faecal samples. The ability of the IgY to detect E. coli in both the fresh and spiked fecal samples highlights its potential for the rapid detection of E. coli in various sample types.

Lastly, the performance of the developed electrochemical method was tested with 10 −1 diluted four different BALB/c mice faecal samples. The observed peak current for the 10 −1 diluted faecal samples were utilized to estimate the concentration of the same using the established cyclic voltammetry calibration line. The found concentration of the endogenous faecal samples were displayed in Table 4. The capacity of the developed electrochemical sensor to quantify the E. coli in direct faecal samples affirms its suitability to effectivity detect E. coli in various sample matrix.

In conclusion, an immunosensor has been successfully developed to enable rapid and precise detection of E. coli in water samples. The modified electrode with B, N-doped GQDs nanointerface facilitated the covalent attachment of the IgY antibody to the electrode surface through EDC/NHS coupling, ensuring efficient transduction of the biosensor. The electrochemical immunosensor exhibited remarkable performance in detecting various concentrations of E. coli, boasting a linear working range of 0.5 × 101 to 107 CFU/mL and a detection limit of 1.69 CFU/mL. Moreover, analysis of endogenous E. coli in fresh faecal samples collected from four different BALB/c mice, and analysis of different water samples, including RO, unprocessed, and processed, was conducted prior to deployment, and the sensor demonstrated effective quantification of endogenous E. coli and satisfactory recoveries in water samples, underscoring its efficacy in real-time E. coli detection. These findings underscore the diagnostic potency of IgY in bacterial sensing applications and, more specifically, establish the developed immunosensor as a promising diagnostic tool for E. coli screening.
